title: Resonance
shortDescription: A climbable, large-scale bamboo queen's conch shell that transforms sound, light, and movement into a shared ritual journey.

heroImage.alt: Resonance — climbable bamboo conch shell installation at dusk

galleryImages.alt: Resonance exterior — bamboo conch shell structure at scale
Interior of Resonance — warm light and climbing structure
Top-down view of Resonance spiral structure
Coastal entry concept for Resonance
Participants moving through Resonance
Resonance interior atmosphere

overviewLead: Resonance invites people into deeper awareness through immersive architecture, sound, light, and collective movement.
overviewBody: Rooted in the geometry of the queen's conch shell and built entirely from bamboo, Resonance is designed to be entered, climbed, listened to, and shared. The experience is created collectively rather than observed from a distance — participants spiral through stairs, nets, ladders, and platforms, emerging at the top with a shared sense of place and presence.
leadArtistBio: Founder and project lead. Creative direction, vision, and overall project development.

collaborators.name: Ed Wilkes
collaborators.role: Structural design and parametric modeling. Engineering development and physical modeling studies.

collaborators.name: Luis Echeverria
collaborators.role: Architectural construction and fabrication coordination with bamboo build partners.

updates.text: Resonance Network website launched — project page is live.
Physical scale model completed and photographed.
Digital parametric model finalized with structural analysis.

experience: Visitors enter through a low, organic opening at the base of the shell and begin spiraling upward through bamboo stairs, rope bridges, net hammocks, and open platforms. Sound and light shift with each level — from deep, resonant tones at the base to airy, wind-driven harmonics near the top. At the summit, an open platform offers panoramic views and a shared sense of arrival. The journey is physical, meditative, and collective — everyone who enters becomes part of the living rhythm of the structure.
artistStory: Resonance was born from a single question: what would it feel like to climb inside a seashell? Elliot Fabri, a multidisciplinary artist and designer, spent years studying the geometry of the queen's conch — its logarithmic spiral, its acoustic properties, its structural elegance. After years of working on large-scale festival installations and immersive environments, Elliot founded Resonance Art Collective to bring this vision to life at permanent, architectural scale. The project is deeply personal — rooted in the belief that built spaces should invite wonder, not just function.
goals: Build a permanent, climbable bamboo installation at architectural scale
Pioneer bamboo construction techniques adapted from traditional Balinese methods
Create an immersive space for 100+ simultaneous visitors
Establish Resonance as a model for artist-led regenerative architecture
Secure fiscal sponsorship and raise $175,000 for Phase 1 construction
location: Bali, Indonesia (primary); festival and exhibition sites worldwide

title: Terra Nexus
shortDescription: A regenerative community pavilion designed to restore degraded urban land through living architecture — integrating constructed wetlands and community gathering space.

heroImage.alt: Terra Nexus — regenerative community pavilion concept

galleryImages.alt: Urban green spaces woven into city infrastructure
Ecological building integrating natural systems
Aerial view of lush landscape meeting open terrain

overviewLead: Terra Nexus transforms degraded urban land into living community infrastructure.
overviewBody: A regenerative community pavilion integrating constructed wetlands, rammed earth walls, and community gathering spaces. Designed to serve as both ecological remediation and social gathering infrastructure.
leadArtistBio: Ecological designer and architect focused on regenerative urban systems.

updates.text: Schematic design finalized — integrating constructed wetland and rammed earth wall systems.
Completed ecological site survey with soil and hydrology analysis.

experience: Approaching Terra Nexus, visitors first hear water — the soft movement of constructed wetlands filtering stormwater through gravel beds and native plantings. The pavilion's rammed earth walls radiate warmth, their layered strata telling the geological story of the site. Inside, the gathering space opens to a living roof where rainwater is visible moving through the system. The air smells of wet earth and native grasses. Community members gather on built-in earthen benches for workshops, meals, and conversation — surrounded by the quiet evidence that damaged land can be brought back to life.
artistStory: Jordan Clarke grew up watching vacant lots in their Detroit neighborhood accumulate trash and standing water. Trained as both an ecologist and an architect, Jordan became obsessed with the question: what if the most neglected urban land could become the most cherished? Terra Nexus emerged from a decade of community work — mapping brownfield sites, running soil remediation workshops, and designing small-scale rain gardens with neighbors. This project is the culmination of that journey: a full-scale demonstration that regenerative infrastructure can be beautiful, functional, and community-owned.
goals: Remediate a brownfield site through constructed wetland systems
Create a year-round community gathering pavilion with passive climate control
Demonstrate rammed earth construction using on-site soil
Establish a replicable model for regenerative community infrastructure
Engage 200+ residents in participatory design and construction

title: Luminous Currents
shortDescription: A kinetic light sculpture for public waterfront spaces — hundreds of solar-powered responsive light elements that move with wind and water currents.

heroImage.alt: Luminous Currents — kinetic light sculpture for waterfront spaces

galleryImages.alt: Luminous light patterns across dramatic landscape
Night installation with ethereal light effects
Immersive atmospheric space with golden light

overviewLead: Luminous Currents animates the interface between water, wind, and light.
overviewBody: 300–500 slender stainless steel masts rise from a waterfront plaza, each topped with a solar-powered LED element that responds to wind and water sensor data in real time.
leadArtistBio: Artist and engineer working at the intersection of kinetic sculpture and ecological data visualization.

updates.text: Firmware for solar-powered LED response system tested with wind and water sensor inputs.
Structural engineering review complete — stainless steel mast design validated.

experience: At dusk, the waterfront transforms. Hundreds of slender steel masts begin to glow — soft points of light that sway and pulse in response to wind gusts and tidal data from sensors below the surface. The effect is oceanic: a field of bioluminescent organisms breathing with the harbor. Visitors walk among the masts, their movement triggering subtle shifts in color temperature. On calm nights the light is steady and meditative; during storms, the entire field surges with intensity. The installation makes invisible natural forces — wind, current, tide — tangible and luminous.
artistStory: Ingrid Voss trained as a mechanical engineer before pivoting to art, drawn by a desire to make ecological data felt rather than read. Growing up on the Dutch coast, she watched the relationship between water, wind, and human infrastructure with an engineer's eye and an artist's heart. Her early kinetic sculptures were small — wind-driven mobiles and sensor-reactive light objects. Luminous Currents is her most ambitious project: an attempt to build an instrument that lets an entire waterfront breathe visibly, translating the invisible rhythms of coastal ecology into shared public experience.
goals: Install 300–500 solar-powered kinetic light elements on a public waterfront
Visualize real-time wind and tidal data through responsive light behavior
Achieve fully off-grid operation through integrated solar harvesting
Create a permanent public artwork that deepens connection to coastal ecology
Secure a municipal public art commission or percent-for-art placement

title: Mycelium Sound Chamber
shortDescription: An acoustic installation where material research and sensory architecture converge into a single living atmosphere.

heroImage.alt: Mycelium Sound Chamber — organic acoustic installation

galleryImages.alt: Natural bamboo and organic material textures
Dense forest atmosphere with dappled light
Contemporary organic art forms and textures

overviewLead: A new kind of acoustic space grown from living materials.
overviewBody: The Mycelium Sound Chamber explores how fungal networks can be shaped into acoustic architecture — combining material science, acoustic engineering, and sensory design into a single immersive environment.
leadArtistBio: Artist and material researcher working with biological materials in architectural contexts.

updates.text: Mycelium panel acoustic testing shows promising sound absorption properties at lab scale.
Growth protocol for architectural-scale mycelium panels established with research partners.

experience: The chamber entrance is a threshold — crossing from the noise of the outside world into a space that absorbs sound in a way that feels almost biological. The walls are alive: grown from mycelium composites, their surfaces are soft, irregular, and faintly warm to the touch. Sound behaves differently here — spoken words dissolve quickly, footsteps vanish, and what remains is a deep, enveloping quiet punctuated by carefully composed ambient tones that seem to emerge from the material itself. Visitors report feeling held, as if the space is listening back.
artistStory: Sera Okafor's path to the Mycelium Sound Chamber began in a university mycology lab, where she was researching the structural properties of fungal networks for her architecture thesis. She became captivated by a simple observation: mycelium composites absorbed sound in ways that synthetic acoustic panels could not. What started as a material science inquiry became an artistic obsession — could you grow a space that fundamentally changed how people experienced sound? Sera left her academic position to pursue this question full-time, founding a studio practice that bridges material research and immersive art.
goals: Grow architectural-scale acoustic panels from mycelium composites
Achieve measurable acoustic performance exceeding conventional materials
Create a fully immersive sound installation using only biological materials
Publish material research findings in collaboration with university partners
Tour the installation to 3+ venues over two years

title: Canopy Commons
shortDescription: A public shade and gathering system designed for workshops, community rituals, and civic activation.

heroImage.alt: Canopy Commons — public shade and gathering structure

galleryImages.alt: Elegant pavilion structure in public space
Community gathering space with warm atmosphere
Public art installation in urban setting

overviewLead: A modular shade system that activates public space for community gathering and civic life.
overviewBody: Canopy Commons is a modular tensile shade system designed for deployment in underserved public spaces — parks, plazas, and street corners — creating covered areas for workshops, markets, ceremonies, and everyday gathering.
leadArtistBio: Architect and community organizer focused on equitable public space design.

updates.text: Community needs assessment completed across three target neighborhoods.
Initial concept sketches shared with community advisory group for feedback.

experience: Under the canopy, the quality of public space changes immediately. Shade transforms a sun-blasted plaza into a place people want to stay. The tensile fabric filters light into soft, warm tones, and the modular steel structure creates a sense of architectural presence without walls. On market days, vendors set up beneath the canopy's bays. On weekday mornings, elders gather for coffee. On weekends, the space hosts workshops, performances, and community meetings. The structure is simple enough to be erected by community members in a day, but designed with enough craft and intention to feel permanent and cared for.
artistStory: Marcus Osei spent fifteen years as a practicing architect watching his firm design beautiful public spaces that nobody from his own neighborhood would ever use. The disconnect between who designs public infrastructure and who needs it most became the central question of his practice. He left his firm to work directly with underserved communities in South Los Angeles, running shade audits that mapped which neighborhoods had tree cover and which had none. Canopy Commons emerged from those audits — a direct response to the reality that in many neighborhoods, there is simply nowhere to gather outside without baking in the sun.
goals: Deploy modular shade structures in 3 underserved public spaces
Design a system that can be erected by community members without heavy equipment
Create a toolkit for community-led deployment in other cities
Reduce peak surface temperatures by 15–20°F in deployment areas
Host 50+ community events under Canopy Commons structures in the first year

title: Solaris Pavilion
shortDescription: A solar-responsive gathering structure that tracks light cycles, generating energy while shading public space below.

galleryImages.alt: Modern architectural structure with clean geometric lines
Contemporary structure with dramatic light and shadow
Dramatic landscape with expansive sky and light

overviewLead: A structure that generates its own energy while serving as a public gathering place.
overviewBody: The Solaris Pavilion uses a solar-tracking canopy system to shade public space while generating renewable energy. The structure responds to daily and seasonal light cycles, creating a dynamic relationship between architecture, climate, and community.
leadArtistBio: Architect and sustainable systems designer focused on energy-generating public infrastructure.

updates.text: Structural and mechanical engineering for canopy tracking system complete.
Solar panel integration approach finalized — moving to prototype phase.

experience: Standing beneath the Solaris Pavilion, you become aware of time passing in a way buildings rarely allow. The canopy panels shift throughout the day, tracking the sun's arc — casting moving shadow patterns across the gathering space below. In the morning, the panels tilt to let warm light through for a farmers market. By midday, they flatten to maximum shade, and the space beneath feels cool and sheltered. As evening approaches, the panels angle to capture the last light, and the pavilion's integrated lighting slowly takes over, powered entirely by the energy harvested during the day. The building breathes with the sun.
artistStory: Yuki Tanaka grew up in a small town in Kyushu, Japan, where her grandparents' home had no air conditioning — instead, it was designed to work with the sun and wind. When she moved to Phoenix, Arizona for architecture school, she was shocked by how American public spaces fought against climate rather than working with it. Solaris Pavilion is her answer: a public structure that doesn't just withstand the sun but collaborates with it. Yuki spent three years developing the solar-tracking canopy mechanism, iterating through dozens of prototypes in her garage workshop before the system achieved the elegance and reliability she demanded.
goals: Build a net-positive energy public pavilion generating more power than it consumes
Develop a solar-tracking canopy system adaptable to different climates
Create comfortable public gathering space in extreme heat environments
Provide free renewable energy charging stations for community use
Demonstrate that public infrastructure can be both energy-generating and beautiful

title: Deep Current
shortDescription: An underwater-inspired immersive environment using projection, sound, and kinetic fabric to evoke tidal movement.

galleryImages.alt: Immersive art installation with flowing light and form
Fabric installation with undulating movement and light
Installation art with atmospheric depth and immersion

overviewLead: An immersive ocean experience built from light, sound, and moving fabric.
overviewBody: Deep Current creates a complete sensory environment inspired by the deep ocean — a space where visitors feel submerged in movement, light, and sound without water. Kinetic fabric structures move in choreographed patterns while spatial audio and projection create the sensation of being within a living tidal system.
leadArtistBio: Interdisciplinary artist working with immersive environments and aquatic ecosystems as artistic material.

updates.text: Kinetic fabric prototype tested — choreographed movement patterns validated.
Spatial audio design complete — 24-channel immersive sound system specified.

experience: Entering Deep Current is like descending into the ocean without getting wet. The space is dark, vast, and enveloping. Massive fabric structures — translucent and subtly lit from within — move in slow, choreographed undulations overhead, mimicking the motion of kelp forests and jellyfish. A 24-channel spatial audio system fills the room with deep, shifting tidal sounds: whale song, currents, the creak of pressure at depth. Projection maps bioluminescent patterns across the floor and walls, responding to visitors' movement. The experience is both deeply calming and awe-inducing — a reminder of the immensity and beauty of the world beneath the surface.
artistStory: Amara Diallo grew up on the coast of Senegal, where the ocean was both livelihood and mythology. She learned to freedive before she learned to read, and the underwater world shaped her sense of what art could be — immersive, enveloping, and felt in the body. After studying sculpture and digital media in Paris, Amara began creating immersive environments that translated her ocean experiences for landlocked audiences. Deep Current is her most personal work: an attempt to give people who may never dive the feeling of being held by the sea — and through that feeling, to build empathy for marine ecosystems under threat.
goals: Create a 5,000+ sq ft immersive ocean environment using kinetic fabric and spatial audio
Develop a touring-ready installation system that can adapt to different venue sizes
Partner with marine conservation organizations to integrate educational content
Reach 50,000+ visitors across a multi-city exhibition tour
Raise $120,000 for fabrication and initial venue partnership
location: Dakar, Senegal (studio); touring to New York, London, and Tokyo

title: Tidal Pavilion
shortDescription: A coastal gathering structure shaped by ecology, ritual use, and public encounter at the edge of land and sea.

heroImage.alt: Tidal Pavilion — coastal gathering structure at the edge of land and sea

galleryImages.alt: Minimal coastal architecture with clean lines and open sky
Landscape architecture at the boundary of land and water
Resort pavilion structure with coastal setting and water views

overviewLead: A structure that belongs to the tidal edge — shaped by the rhythms of land and sea.
overviewBody: The Tidal Pavilion proposes a coastal gathering space whose form emerges from tidal ecology and human ritual. Designed for communities at the water's edge, it frames encounter, ceremony, and daily use within an architecture that acknowledges the dynamism of coastal environments.
leadArtistBio: Architect and cultural practitioner focused on Pacific coastal communities and tidal ecology.

updates.text: Cultural research phase complete — documenting Pacific coastal community gathering traditions.
Initial concept sketches developed — exploring forms shaped by tidal movement.

experience: The Tidal Pavilion is never the same twice. At low tide, the structure sits on exposed rock and sand, its open platforms serving as gathering space for community meals, storytelling, and ceremony. As the tide rises, water enters the lower chambers through designed channels, creating shallow reflecting pools that mirror the sky. At high tide, the pavilion becomes a promontory — visitors stand at the edge of architecture and ocean, spray on their skin, watching the water claim and release the space. The building doesn't resist the tide; it choreographs with it, teaching visitors to read the rhythms of the coast through their bodies.
artistStory: Raia Fatu was raised in a fishing village in Samoa, where the boundary between land and sea was never fixed. Buildings were designed to withstand cyclones, ceremonies timed to tidal cycles, and the ocean was understood not as a threat but as a relative. When Raia studied architecture in New Zealand, she found that coastal design was dominated by defensive thinking — seawalls, barriers, retreat. The Tidal Pavilion is her counter-proposal: an architecture that works with tidal systems rather than against them, drawing on Pacific Indigenous knowledge of coastal living that has sustained communities for millennia.
goals: Design a coastal structure that integrates tidal ecology into its architecture
Embed Pacific Indigenous knowledge of coastal living into contemporary design
Create a year-round community gathering space at the tidal edge
Develop a replicable approach to tidal-responsive architecture
Engage a Pacific coastal community as full partners in the design process
location: Apia, Samoa (primary site); Pacific coastal communities

title: Root Network
shortDescription: A living infrastructure project embedding ecological corridors into urban neighborhoods through art and participatory design.

heroImage.alt: Root Network — ecological corridors woven into urban neighborhoods

galleryImages.alt: Sculpture garden with organic forms in green setting
Art gallery corridor with curated environmental works
Geometric architecture integrated with urban green space

overviewLead: Embedding ecological corridors into the fabric of urban neighborhoods.
overviewBody: Root Network is a participatory infrastructure project that works with urban communities to design and install ecological corridors — continuous green and permeable pathways that connect fragmented urban nature. The project uses art, participatory design, and community engagement to embed ecological thinking into neighborhood planning.
leadArtistBio: Landscape architect and community organizer specializing in urban ecological design and grassroots infrastructure.

updates.text: Corridor route design finalized across three urban blocks with community input.
Completed second round of community design workshops with 40+ participants.

experience: Walking a Root Network corridor feels different from walking an ordinary sidewalk. The ground is permeable — rainwater soaks in rather than running off. Native plantings line both sides, buzzing with pollinators that have returned since the corridor was installed. Murals and sculptural markers designed by neighborhood residents tell the story of each block. Children use the corridors as safe walking routes to school. Elders tend small garden plots embedded in the pathway. The corridor is infrastructure, art, ecology, and community space woven into a single continuous thread through the neighborhood — proof that urban nature and urban life can share the same ground.
artistStory: Fatima Al-Hassan grew up in a neighborhood in East Oakland where the nearest park was a 20-minute bus ride away, but vacant lots were on every block. She became a landscape architect specifically to answer the question: why do some neighborhoods have nature and others don't? After a decade of community organizing and design work, Fatima realized that isolated parks weren't enough — neighborhoods needed continuous ecological infrastructure woven into their daily paths. Root Network is the synthesis of her life's work: a design framework that embeds ecological corridors into the fabric of underserved neighborhoods, one block at a time, designed by the people who live there.
goals: Install ecological corridors across 6 urban blocks in a pilot neighborhood
Increase permeable surface area by 30% in the pilot zone
Engage 300+ residents in participatory design and installation
Create a replicable toolkit for community-led ecological corridor design
Document measurable improvements in biodiversity, stormwater capture, and urban heat
